1.synthesis: The Davidic sons list opens with the six sons born at Hebron (vv1-4) — Amnon, Daniel/Chileab, Absalom, Adonijah, Shephatiah, Ithream — and continues with the sons born at Jerusalem (vv5-8), including four sons of Bathsheba (bat-šûaʿ, bat-shua): Shimea, Shobab, Nathan, and Solomon. Nine additional Jerusalem sons are named (vv6-8: Ibhar through Eliphelet), with the Elishama and Eliphelet names each appearing twice — possibly indicating sons who died young and were memorialized by naming a later son after them, or variant textual traditions. The section closes with the note that Tamar was David’s daughter (v9), listing her alongside the sons — an unusual genealogical inclusion that preserves her identity as a named person within the royal household despite (or because of) the violation she suffered. The Chronicler omits that narrative entirely while honoring her by name.
1.historical_context: David’s Hebron wives represent diplomatic alliances: Ahinoam of Jezreel (local Judahite), Abigail the Carmelite (significant social match), Maacah daughter of Talmai king of Geshur (Transjordanian political alliance — her son Absalom inherited Geshurite royal blood), Haggith, Abital, and Eglah. The Jerusalem sons include four sons of “Bathshua daughter of Ammiel” — the same person as Bathsheba daughter of Eliam in 2 Samuel 11:3 (the names Ammiel and Eliam are inverted forms of each other: “people of God” vs “God of people”). Nathan son of David (v5) appears in Luke’s genealogy of Jesus as the ancestor through whom the Messianic line descended via Mary (Luke 3:31), providing the non-Solomonic line. Tamar’s inclusion in the list alongside the sons is without parallel in the Samuel narrative.
1.literary_structure: The sons-of-David section is structured biographically: Hebron sons (six, with mothers named, vv1-4) → summary of Hebron reign duration (v4b) → Jerusalem sons (Solomon last of Bathsheba’s four, then nine more, vv5-8) → closing note on Tamar (v9). The mothers are named for the Hebron sons but not the Jerusalem sons — a genealogical pattern that prioritizes provenance when multiple political alliances are at stake. Solomon is the climactic name in the Jerusalem list, appearing after his three brothers by Bathsheba — the Chronicler’s narrative will focus heavily on Solomon as temple-builder, making his genealogical positioning significant.
1.theological_application: The multiplication of David’s sons — six at Hebron, thirteen or more at Jerusalem — is the genealogical enactment of the Davidic covenant’s expansiveness: YHWH promised to establish David’s house and kingdom forever (2 Samuel 7:16), and the list of sons demonstrates that the royal household was flourishing. None of the palace intrigues that surrounded these sons (Amnon’s death, Absalom’s rebellion, Adonijah’s bid, Solomon’s consolidation) appear in the genealogy — only names and mothers. The Chronicler’s theological decision is to honor the covenant’s genealogical fulfillment without rehearsing the failures that surrounded it.
1.christology: Two Davidic genealogical lines matter for the New Testament’s Christology: the Solomon-to-Joseph line in Matthew (Matthew 1:6-16), establishing Jesus’ legal claim through Joseph, and the Nathan-to-Mary line in Luke (Luke 3:31), establishing Jesus’ physical descent. Both lines run through sons of David listed here in verse 5. Matthew traces the royal-legal claim; Luke traces the biological descent. The Chronicler’s list of Bathsheba’s four sons — Nathan and Solomon among them — is the genealogical source for both evangelists’ different ways of documenting the same claim: Jesus is the heir of David.

10.synthesis: The royal succession from Solomon to Zedekiah unfolds in seven verses: Solomon → Rehoboam → Abijah → Asa → Jehoshaphat → Joram → Ahaziah → Joash → Amaziah → Azariah → Jotham → Ahaz → Hezekiah → Manasseh → Amon → Josiah. Josiah’s four sons are named with their birth order (vv15-16): Johanan the firstborn, Jehoiakim the second, Zedekiah the third, Shallum the fourth. The note that Jeconiah (“the captive”, v17) was the son of Jehoiakim rather than a direct son of Josiah is implicit in the list’s compression. The seventeen-generation sequence from Solomon to Zedekiah represents four centuries of the Judahite monarchy, with each name carrying narrative weight that the genealogy suppresses in favor of the pure dynastic chain.
10.historical_context: The succession runs with minimal deviation from the Samuel-Kings narrative, though two anomalies appear: (1) Azariah in v12 is the same as Uzziah (his throne name) who reigned 52 years — the Chronicler’s narrative will call him Uzziah consistently; (2) Josiah’s sons are listed in birth order (Johanan, Jehoiakim, Zedekiah, Shallum), but the reign order was different — Jehoahaz/Shallum (the fourth son) reigned first, then Jehoiakim (the second), then Jehoiachin/Jeconiah (not listed as a son here, but Jehoiakim’s son, v17), then Zedekiah (the third). The non-birth-order reigns reflect Egyptian and Babylonian imperial manipulation of Judah’s succession in the kingdom’s final century. The Chronicler preserves both the birth order and the genealogical connections without resolving the chronological sequence.
10.literary_structure: The king list uses the same succession formula throughout (X begot Y) without evaluative comment — a striking contrast to Kings, which grades every monarch by the standard of David. The Chronicler’s genealogical format strips evaluation entirely: the dynastic chain is the point, not the moral history of each reign. Josiah’s four sons and their death/captivity fates (implied by the sequence) bring the monarchy to its collapse point, setting up verse 17’s transition to the exilic and post-exilic Davidic line.
10.theological_application: The seventeen-generation sequence from Solomon to Zedekiah is the Chronicler’s compressed documentation of four centuries of covenant faithfulness and failure — compressed because the genealogy’s purpose is to show the line continued, not to evaluate each link. The line survived Athaliah’s usurpation (the six-year temple hiding of Joash/Jehoash, 2 Chronicles 22:10–23:3), Ahaz’s apostasy, and Manasseh’s fifty-five-year apostasy followed by repentance (2 Chronicles 33:10-17). Each name in the list represents YHWH’s faithfulness in maintaining the covenant even when the covenant’s human partners repeatedly violated it.
10.christology: Matthew’s genealogy of Jesus includes fourteen of these kings in its second section (Matthew 1:7-11: Solomon through Jeconiah) and notes that there were “fourteen generations from David to the deportation to Babylon.” Matthew’s deliberate organization of the genealogy into three groups of fourteen — tracing the covenant’s arc from Abraham through the monarchy, through the exile, to Christ — makes the Chronicler’s king list the backbone of the second section. Every Judahite king who failed to embody mišpāṭ ûṣĕḏāqāh (justice and righteousness) is a negative witness to the one king who will permanently fulfill it: the Branch who “will execute justice and righteousness in the land” (Jeremiah 23:5).

17.pericope_label: The Royal Line After the Exile
17.synthesis: Jeconiah “the captive” (hāʾāsîr, v17) stands at the exile’s hinge point. From him descend Shealtiel, Malchiram, Pedaiah, Shenazzar, Jekamiah, Hoshama, and Nedabiah — the first generation in Babylon. Zerubbabel son of Pedaiah (or Shealtiel in the other genealogical traditions, v19) is the post-exilic Davidic heir who governed Yehud under the Persians. His sons (Meshullam, Hananiah, and their sister Shelomith, plus five more sons in v20) continue the line into the late Persian period. The genealogy extends further through Hananiah’s sons (Pelatiah, Jeshaiah) and three more generations (Rephaiah, Arnan, Obadiah, Shecaniah) before reaching Shecaniah’s sons and Elioenai’s seven sons — the latest generation the Chronicler can document, probably his own contemporaries in Persian-period Yehud.
17.historical_context: Jeconiah/Jehoiachin was deported to Babylon in 597 BCE and eventually released by Evil-merodach (2 Kings 25:27-30); his “captive” epithet in the genealogy encodes the trauma of 597 into the genealogical record permanently. Zerubbabel served as Persian-appointed governor of Yehud under Darius I (ca. 522-486 BCE), attested in Haggai and Zechariah. Haggai designates him YHWH’s ḥôtām (signet ring, Haggai 2:23) — explicitly reversing the curse on Jeconiah who was torn off as a signet ring (Jeremiah 22:24). The six-generation extension beyond Zerubbabel (Hananiah → Pelatiah/Jeshaiah → Rephaiah → Arnan → Obadiah → Shecaniah → Elioenai) at approximately 25 years per generation places the Chronicler’s composition ca. 400-350 BCE — making the final names likely living contemporaries.
17.literary_structure: The post-exilic section follows the same X begot Y formula as the monarchy section but now documents obscure individuals unknown from any other text. This genealogical care for the unnamed is the Chronicler’s most pointed theological statement about the exile: captivity did not end the Davidic line, and the line’s continuation through ordinary people whose names appear in no other biblical text is itself a form of covenant witness. The final verse (v24) lists seven sons for Elioenai — mirroring the seven sons of Jesse who preceded David — as a literary gesture toward completion.
17.theological_application: The post-exilic Davidic genealogy is the Chronicler’s most courageous theological act: he traces a royal line that has no king, no throne, no political power, through people whose names are otherwise unknown to history, and presents this as the continuation of YHWH’s covenant with David. The son named Jushab-hesed (“covenant-loyalty has returned,” v20) is a theological confession embedded in a personal name: the child’s parents believed that YHWH’s ḥesed had returned to Israel after the exile. Every generation of obscure Davidic descendants keeping the lineage alive is an act of faith that the covenant will eventually be fulfilled through this line.
17.christology: The genealogy’s extension from Jeconiah/Jehoiachin (“the captive”) through Zerubbabel to the Chronicler’s own contemporaries is the OT’s most extended anticipation of what the NT declares: the covenant did not end at Babylon, and the Davidic heir eventually arrived. Matthew’s third section (Matthew 1:12-16) traces from Jeconiah (“after the deportation to Babylon”) through the obscure post-exilic generations to Joseph — precisely the period the Chronicler documents here. Zerubbabel’s messianic significance in Haggai (Haggai 2:23: “I will make you like a signet ring, for I have chosen you”) is the OT’s clearest anticipation of the resurrection-vindication that would establish the true Davidic heir permanently: “declared to be the Son of God in power according to the Spirit of holiness by his resurrection from the dead” (Romans 1:4).
